does: This paper introduces PaperGuard, the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating the robustness of multimodal AI systems used in scientific peer review against adversarial attacks. The work constructs a dataset of multimodal scientific papers across multiple domains and develops a unified attack suite targeting domain-specific failures in AI reviewers, including black-box prompt injections and white-box perturbations on both text and image modalities. The authors demonstrate that current AI peer-review systems are vulnerable to these attacks and propose corresponding defenses, showing that this vulnerability differs fundamentally from standard jailbreaking due to its focus on inducing targeted review-specific failures rather than general safety violations.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about deploying LLMs and MLLMs in high-stakes domains like scientific publishing, extending prior robustness research that has focused primarily on text-only inputs. It responds to the broader adversarial robustness literature for multimodal models but applies it to the novel domain of peer review, where the stakes and attack incentives differ from general-purpose model jailbreaking. The paper connects to earlier work on vision-language model vulnerabilities and prompt injection attacks, while also drawing on the emerging field of AI-assisted scientific evaluation, recognizing that scientific papers inherently combine textual arguments and visual evidence in ways that existing single-modality attack frameworks cannot capture.
enables: This benchmark enables future research into defenses specifically tailored for domain-critical AI systems, moving beyond generic adversarial robustness toward application-aware security. It opens investigation into what visual features (figures, tables, plots) are most susceptible to manipulation in review contexts and how reviewers might verify authenticity of supporting evidence. The work positions multimodal peer review as a test bed for more broadly securing high-stakes AI deployment, potentially informing defenses for other critical domains involving document understanding. It suggests paths toward developing AI-assisted review systems that can detect when papers or their visualizations have been adversarially crafted, and toward creating reviewer guidelines for hybrid human-AI workflows that remain secure under targeted manipulation.
